articles.title: Brickbat: Color Blind
articles.content: 在佛罗里达州沃卢西亚县，林赛·艾萨克斯被逮捕并被指控八项重罪，包括车辆致死、肇事逃逸和鲁莽驾驶，此前调查人员错误地指控她造成了一起导致三人死亡的撞车事故。调查人员在被撞车辆上发现了栗色油漆，一名目击者称嫌疑人的车辆是栗色的，并提供了一个与艾萨克斯车辆不符的部分车牌号——她的是一辆没有可见损坏的黑色道奇Durango。尽管如此，调查人员根据Flock Safety车牌识别系统将她锁定为嫌疑人，认定她在事发时出现在该区域。她在监狱里被关押了13天后，检察官最终撤销了所有指控，此前艾萨克斯的律师利用时空距离分析证明她不可能在事故发生时出现在现场。当局随后指控了另一名女性艾莉莎·李·蒙塔尔沃，他们称她当时驾驶的是一辆栗色道奇Durango。
articles.summary:
In Volusia County, Florida, Lindsey Isaacs was arrested and charged with eight felony counts, including vehicular homicide, leaving the scene of a deadly crash, and reckless driving, after investigators wrongly accused her of causing a crash that killed three people. Investigators found maroon paint on the struck vehicle, while a witness said the suspect's vehicle was maroon and provided a partial license plate number that did not match Isaacs' vehicle, a black Dodge Durango with no visible damage. Nonetheless, investigators identified Isaacs as the suspect because Flock Safety license plate readers placed her in the area at the time. She spent 13 days in jail before prosecutors finally dropped all charges, after Isaacs' attorney used time-distance analysis to prove she could not have been at the scene at the time of the crash. Authorities eventually charged another woman, Alisa Lee Montalvo, whom they say was driving a maroon Dodge Durango.

The post Brickbat: Color Blind appeared first on Reason.com.

articles.title: Open Thread
articles.content:
**沃尔科赫阴谋**
多为法学教授 | 时有逆向观点 | 常持自由至上主义立场 | 始终保持独立

如此多的媒体试图告诉你该思考什么。支持帮助你独立思考的新闻报道。

articles.title: The Stains On The Federal Judiciary
articles.content:
联邦司法机构的污点

一位高级政府官员在工作场所多次发生性关系。一块布料被弄脏。该官员在被问及发生的事情时，在正式调查中撒谎。但多亏了一位举报人，真相大白。该官员被抓了现行后道歉，并坚称此类事件绝不会再发生。听起来很熟悉？

克林顿总统与莫妮卡·莱温斯基的故事对所有35岁以上的人来说都不陌生。（我记得14岁时用56k调制解调器下载了斯塔尔报告。）莱温斯基是一名实习生，在白宫与克林顿发生了十次性接触。大多数会面发生在椭圆形办公室，虽然有些发生在特朗普总统目前用来展示MAGA周边商品的房间里。莱温斯基从未与克林顿发生性交，但他们确实有过口交。克林顿触摸了莱温斯基的生殖器，并以不当方式使用了一支雪茄。坐在椭圆形办公室外面的秘书贝蒂·库里保持着看似合理的推诿空间，当莱温斯基在里面时会离开该区域。克林顿 famously用他的体液弄脏了莱温斯基从GAP买的蓝色连衣裙。

很快，指控被泄露出去。起初，比尔·克林顿撒了谎。在1998年1月一场 infamous的新闻发布会上，他说：“我和那个女人莱温斯基没有发生性关系。”但琳达·特里普秘密录下了与莱温斯基的对话，并将其提供给独立检察官肯·斯塔尔。直到真相曝光后，克林顿才改变口风。他坚称：“这取决于'是'这个字的含义是什么。”克林顿总统因妨碍司法公正和作伪证（在大陪审团前提供虚假证词）而被弹劾。最终，克林顿被宣判无罪。宣判无罪后，他因在大陪审团面前提供误导性证词而被苏珊·韦伯·赖特法官判定民事藐视法庭罪。克林顿放弃了阿肯色州的律师执照五年。

现在让我们转向第十一巡回法院的“涉案法官”。就目前而言，我会假装我们都不知道这位法官的身份。我今天与几位记者和调查人员交谈过。他们都同意我的分析。名字很快就会曝光。现在，我只能将这位可耻的法官称为贝齐法官。

贝齐法官与一名执法人员在她的小办公室里发生过多次性关系，时间持续了一年多。这名执法人员的部门经常在该法官面前进行诉讼。这似乎比与实习生发生性关系存在更大的利益冲突风险。与贝蒂·库里不同——贝蒂可以在老板发生性关系时走开——贝齐法官可怜的书记员被迫听到“亲吻声”和“呻吟声”。而且，就像莫妮卡的蓝色连衣裙一样，书记员报告说：“涉案法官办公室沙发上的一块垫子被弄脏了，痕迹与精液造成的痕迹一致。”

当调查开始时，贝齐法官撒了谎。她说这些指控“令人愤慨”和“毫无根据”并予以否认。然后她攻击了她的书记员——那位举报人——说她迟到、在法庭上使用手机、穿着“太随便”。贝齐法官羞辱书记员的穿着真是厚颜无耻，因为她自己却在办公室里脱衣服发生性关系。这一指控为大卫·拉特的老博客“法袍之下”赋予了新的含义。值得注意的是，贝齐法官将其书记员的行为归咎为对终身任职的联邦法官的报复。权力关系完全颠倒过来了。

贝齐法官向首席地区法官发送了两封后续邮件。她撒谎说：“我感到震惊和困惑，根本不知道这位书记员指的是什么，也不知道她为什么提出这样的指控。”二十分钟后，她又撒了另一个谎：“我甚至不知道这位书记员指的是哪名执法人员。”贝齐法官向普赖尔首席法官撒谎说：“我从未在办公室或法院的任何其他地方发生过性关系。”贝齐法官完全可以从比尔·克林顿嘴里借用这些话。正如克林顿一样，贝齐法官因为一位举报人而落网。书记员通过内部渠道报告了这一事件。谢天谢地，没有秘密录音。

不幸的是，相似之处仅此而已。克林顿被弹劾、被判藐视法庭罪、放弃了律师执照。贝齐法官只是被轻轻警告了一下，而且她的身份被保密。她的惩罚是：她可以写一封措辞含糊的道歉信，不会承认任何真正的错误；她不必参加那些可能被视为奖励的繁琐委员会；而且她可以免除担任首席法官的所有繁琐行政工作。贝齐法官甚至不阅读她签署的70%的民事命令；这在我看来不是一个特别称职的管理者。（我查了她的案件列表，她今天和昨天都签署了命令；世界继续运转。）根本没有任何惩罚。她妨碍了调查、向多位法官撒谎、并试图对书记员进行报复。她只是在被抓后才道歉。

联邦司法机构有很多污点。贝齐法官办公室沙发垫上有一块污点。第十一巡回法院司法委员会的污点在于公然滥用自由裁量权，将这一谴责保密。司法会议司法行为与 disability委员会的污点在于确认了这一保密谴责。我尊重这两个机构中的一些法官，但他们在这里犯了严重的错误。

当然，最大的污点是由司法机器的每一位成员留下的，他们什么都没有做，以阻止对宝琳·纽曼法官的隐性弹劾。法官们可以在办公室里发生性关系、报复书记员、撒谎，但可以将谴责保密。但什么错都没有犯的纽曼法官多年来无法审理案件。穆尔甚至通过在一幅自恋的人工智能卡通中省略纽曼来嘲讽她。所有人都应该感到羞耻。

接下来会发生什么？

首先，众议院需要对贝齐法官启动弹劾程序。也许如果贝齐法官感受到真正的压力，她会采取体面的方式辞职。此外，如果司法机构认为有必要将整个事件保密，国会可以照亮整个事件。弹劾权应包括从司法委员会传唤记录的权力。作为一个 general matter，司法记录不应受到弹劾的约束，但在这里，法官们是根据联邦法规以行政身份行事的。根据特朗普诉马扎斯案，国会似乎有权调查其法律如何对现任联邦法官执行。如果司法机构认真想要避免司法弹劾，那么他们需要认真对待自身内部的misconduct。

其次，国会需要重新审视给予联邦法官自我监管的自由裁量权。如果这些事实可以合理地作为保密谴责的理由，那么规则需要改变。公众需要知道。请记住，穆尔法官试图使用“匿名”来将其对纽曼法官的调查保密。传票也可以用于推动新的司法道德立法。我意识到司法改革的大部分问题都是党派问题，但我希望所有公正的人都会同意，对纽曼法官和贝齐法官的惩罚并不相似。

第三，佐治亚州律师协会需要对贝齐法官展开纪律处分程序，她仍然是具有良好的信誉 active member。毕竟，在我们看到的所有“律师资格”闹剧之后，这将是一个特别 appropriate的目标，纪律顾问可以调查。一个在办公室里发生性关系，然后对其主持法官撒谎的联邦法官，是否具有执业律师的 candor 和 character？

第四，鉴于这位法官与获胜的地区检察官有着长期的友谊——后者自己在工作场所的不忠方面也有一些经验——富尔顿县地方检察官不太可能采取任何行动。佐治亚州北区联邦检察官可能会发现这位法官的虚假陈述和滥用政府财产（那个垫子）值得调查。
articles.summary:
A high-ranking government official has sexual relations in the workplace on multiple occasions. An article of fabric is stained. The official is asked about what happened and lies during an official investigation. But thanks to a whistleblower, the truth comes out. The official, caught in flagrante delicto, apologizes and insists it will never happen again. Sound familiar?

The saga of President Bill Clinton and Monica Lewinsky is well known to everyone over the age of 35. (I remember downloading the Starr Report over my 56k modem when I was 14 years old.) Lewinsky, an intern, had ten sexual encounters with Clinton in the White House. Most of the meetings occurred in the Oval Office, though some occurred in the room President Trump currently uses to display MAGA swag. Lewinsky never had sexual intercourse with Clinton, but they did have oral sex. Clinton touched Lewinsky's genitals and used a cigar in an inappropriate fashion. Betty Currie, the secretary who sat outside the Oval Office, maintained her plausible deniability, and would leave the area when Lewinsky was in there. Clinton infamously stained Lewinsky's blue dress from the GAP with his fluids.

Soon, the allegations leaked out. Initially, Bill Clinton lied. In an infamous January 1998 press conference, he said "I did not have sexual relations with that woman, Ms. Lewinsky." But Linda Tripp had secretly recorded conversations with Lewinsky, and provided them to Independent Counsel Ken Starr. Only after the truth came out did Clinton change his tune. He insisted, "It depends on what the meaning of the word 'is' is." President Clinton was impeached for obstruction of justice and perjury for giving false testimony in the Lewinsky investigation. Ultimately, Clinton was acquitted. After his acquittal, he was held in civil contempt by Judge Susan Webber Wright for misleading testimony before the grand jury. Clinton surrendered his Arkansas law license for five years.

Now lets turn to the "subject judge" in the Eleventh Circuit. For present purposes, I will pretend that we don't all know the judge's identity. I've spoken to several journalists and investigators today. They all concur with my analysis. The name will come out soon enough. For now, I'll just refer to this disgraced jurist as Judge Betsy.

Judge Betsy had sexual relations with a law enforcement officer in her chambers many times over the course of a year. This officer's department routinely litigated in front of the judge. This seems like a far greater risk conflict than sex with an intern. Unlike Betty Currie, who could walk away when her boss was engaging in sexual relations, Judge Betsy's poor law clerk was forced to hear "kissing sounds" and "sounds of moaning." And, much like Monica's blue dress, the clerk reported that "one of the cushions on a sofa in the Subject Judge's chambers was stained in a manner that was consistent with being caused by semen."

When the investigation began, Judge Betsy lied. She said the allegations were "outrageous" and "baseless" and denied them. She then attacked her law clerk, the whistle blower, for being late, using her phone in court, and dressing "too casual." Some chutzpah for Judge Betsy to shame her clerk's attire considering she disrobed to have sex in her chambers. This charge gives new meaning to David Lat's old blog, "underneath their robes." Remarkably, Judge Betsy blamed her law clerk for retaliating against a life-tenured federal judge. The power dynamics are completely backwards.

Judge Betsy sent two follow-up emails to the Chief District Judge. She lied: "I am astounded and confused, and have no idea what this clerk is referring to or why [the clerk] has made such allegations." Twenty minutes later, she told another lie: "I don't even know which law enforcement officer [the clerk] is referring to." Judge Betsy lied to Chief Judge Pryor: "I have never engaged in sexual intercourse in my office, nor anywhere else in the Courthouse." Judge Betsy could have taken those words right out of Bill Clinton's mouth. Much like Clinton, Judge Betsy got caught due to a whistleblower. The clerk reported the message through internal channels. Thankfully, there are no secret recordings.

The similarities, unfortunately, end here. Clinton was impeached, held in contempt, and surrendered his law license. Judge Betsy was given a slap on the wrist, and her identity was kept secret. Her punishment: she can write a vaguely-worded apology letter that will not confess any real errors, she won't have to serve on burdensome committees (that might be seen as a reward), and she can avoid all of the burdensome administrative work of being chief judge. Judge Betsy doesn't even read 70% of the civil orders she signed; this doesn't strike me as a particularly capable administrator. (I checked her docket, and she has signed orders today and yesterday; the world continues to turn.) There is no punishment at all. She obstructed the investigation, lied to several judges, and tried to retaliate against her law clerk. She only apologized when she got caught.

There are many stains on the federal judiciary. There is a stain on the couch cushion in Judge Betsy's chambers. Another stain is the flagrant abuse of discretion by Judicial Council of the Eleventh Circuit to make this reprimand private. Yet another stain was left by the Committee on Judicial Conduct and Disability of the Judicial Conference that affirmed this private reprimand. There are judges on both bodies that I respect, but they grossly erred here.

Of course, the biggest stain has been left by every member of the judicial apparatus who has done NOTHING to stop the stealth impeachment of Pauline Newman by Kimberly Moore. Judges can have sex in their offices, retaliate against their law clerks, lie about it, yet keep their reprimand private. But Judge Newman who has done nothing wrong hasn't been able to hear a case in years. Moore even mocks Newman by omitting her from a narcisisttic AI cartoon. Shame on them all.

What come next?

First, the House of Representatives needs to initiate impeachment proceedings for Judge Betsy. Perhaps if Judge Betsy felt some actual heat, she would take the honorable course and resign. Moreover, if the judiciary feels compelled to keep this entire matter under wraps, Congress can shine light on the entire matter. The impeachment power should include the power to subpoena records from the Judicial Council. As a generally matter, judicial records should not be subject to impeachment, but here the judges are acting in an administrative capacity pursuant to a federal statute. Under Trump v. Mazars, it would seem Congress has the power to investigate how its law is being enforced against a sitting federal judge. If the judiciary is serious about avoiding judicial impeachments, then they need to take seriously policing their own internal misconduct.

Second, Congress needs to revisit the discretion given to federal judges to police themselves. If these facts can conceivably justify keeping a reprimand private, the rules need to be changed. The public needs to know. Remember that Judge Moore tried to use "anonymity" to keep her inquisition of Judge Newman private. The subpoenas could also be used in furtherance of enacting new judicial ethics legislation. I realize much of these issues about judicial reform are partisan, but I hope all fair-minded people would agree that the punishments for Judges Newman and Betsy are not similar.

Third, the Georgia Bar needs to open a disciplinary proceeding into Judge Betsy, who remains an active member in good standing. After all the nonsense "barfare" we have seen, this would be an especially appropriate target for the disciplinary counsel to look into. Does a federal judge who has sex in chambers, and then lies to her presiding judges, have the candor and character to practice law?

Fourth, given the Judge's longtime friendship with the victorious DA, who herself had some experience with infidelity on the job, it is unlikely the Fulton County District Attorney will do anything. The U.S. Attorney for the Northern District of Georgia may find worthy of investigation the Judge's false statements and misuse of government property (that, the cushions).

The post The Stains On The Federal Judiciary appeared first on Reason.com.

articles.title: How the Billionaire Tax Could Make California Poorer
articles.content:
# 亿万富翁税如何让加州更穷

今年11月，加州选民将面对两项相互竞争的税收提案。第一项是“亿万富翁税法案”，对加州最富有人士的累积净资产征收一次性5%的税款。另一项鲜为人知的是“退休和个人储蓄保护法案”，该法案将在宪法层面划定萨克拉门托可以征税和不得征税的范围，禁止对退休账户、个人储蓄和个人资产征收新税，并禁止追溯性征税。

任何略有积蓄的人——不仅仅是亿万富翁税针对的加州超级富豪——都应该理解这两项提案的真正含义。

首先看亿万富翁税法案。其承诺与实际效果之间的差距显而易见。斯坦福大学的约书亚·劳赫与胡佛研究所的同事们进行了详细计算，结果表明该法案能否带来任何税收收入都令人怀疑。

提案支持者声称该税将筹集1000亿美元。但劳赫的团队发现，亿万富翁们早已用脚投票：拉里·埃里森于2020年离开加州，另有六位亿万富翁——包括谷歌联合创始人拉里·佩奇和谢尔盖·布林——在该提案宣布后至2025年12月31日（即税款生效前一天）期间相继离开。

仅这些离开者就使该法案的预期税收收入减少了近40%，而此时还未收到一分钱。将学术文献中发现的迁移模式应用于更多不为人知的离开者后，预期收入仅剩400亿美元。

现在再加上那些离开的亿万富翁将不再缴纳的常规州税。劳赫的团队计算，由于该措施缩小了现有税基，其“净现值”至少让加州损失250亿美元。

还有追溯效力的问题。该提案旨在根据居住地和追溯至1月1日的行为对亿万富翁征税——远在任何投票之前。那些认为自己合法在其他地方设立居所的人，可能要在法庭上与加州抗争多年（费用由剩余纳税人承担），而争论的焦点可能只是这些亿万富翁在哪里养宠物或持有俱乐部会员资格这种任意细节。

对该税“一次性”的定性同样值得质疑。正如劳赫指出的，该提案包含一项宪法授权，可以取消加州对无形资产征税的上限。一旦这种法律基础设施存在，未来的财富税可以在任何时候以任何税率、在任何门槛上征收。换言之，这是赋予州政府的一项永久性新权力。

亿万富翁税法案如此反复无常，其先例又如此成问题，几乎是在恳请加州人关注第二项公投提案。所有美国人的储蓄都应该基于三个明确原则免受这种掠夺。

第一，公平原则：当工作者将税后收入存起来用于投资退休时，产生的余额并非未开发的收入。将这种储蓄视为新的税基等于对同一美元重复征税。

第二，稳定原则：一种针对资产价值而非收入流的税收制度本质上是不稳定的。一位创始人的股票在经济低迷期下跌40%，仍需按去年更高的估值缴纳财富税。普通储户的401(k)被征税时也会面临同样的荒谬。

第三，也是最紧迫的，是加州自己的过往记录。根据州无党派的立法分析师办公室数据，州支出预计将从2019年增长近70%至本财年，大大超过同期显著的收入增长。结果是未来两年累计赤字超过500亿美元，这是萨克拉门托自己造成的，与华盛顿无关。信任有着这样支出记录的政客们会止步于对亿万富翁征税，是鲁莽且天真的。

当财富税不可避免地未能兑现承诺时，州政府就会寻找下一个可用的资产池。在加州的亿万富翁离开后留下的非亿万富翁将成为潜在目标，他们的退休储蓄可能成为新的税基。正如劳赫本月早些时候在对该提案的持续探讨中所写的那样：“虽然约0.001%的加州家庭是亿万富翁，但约62%拥有退休账户。”

如果这个预测听起来因联邦保护而遥不可及——或者你认为亿万富翁将永远与那些用一生时间积累退休账户的普通储户受到不同对待——请想想加州对健康储蓄账户（HSA）已经做了什么。

联邦法律将HSA贡献和收益视为免税。但根据加州的税收设计，利息、股息和资本收益被视为普通收入，影响约450万居民。这些人不是亿万富翁或百万富翁。政客们只是认定这是州政府有权征税的收入。对401(k)和IRA做同样的事情不需要新原则，只需要同样的意愿。

对亿万富翁征收财富税只是第一步，它将普通加州人的退休储蓄置于风险之中。HSA的先例表明这种威胁是真实存在的。“退休和个人储蓄保护法案”将在宪法层面建立屏障，防止这种扩张。

版权2026 CREATORS.COM

本文首次发表于Reason.com
articles.summary:
Californians will face two competing tax measures this November. The first is the Billionaire Tax Act, a onetime, 5 percent levy on the accumulated net worth of the state's richest residents. Lesser known is the Retirement and Personal Savings Protection Act, which would draw constitutional lines around what Sacramento can and cannot tax, prohibiting new levies on retirement accounts, personal savings, and individually owned assets and banning retroactive taxation.

Everyone with even just a little bit of money set aside—not just the California billionaires targeted by the wealth tax—should understand what these two measures represent.

Start with the Billionaire Tax Act. The gap between what it promises and what it would deliver is stark. Joshua Rauh of Stanford University has run the numbers with his Hoover Institution colleagues, and the results cast doubt on the prospect of any revenue gain whatsoever.

Proponents claim the tax would raise $100 billion. Rauh's team found that billionaires have already been voting with their feet: Larry Ellison left California in 2020, and six others, including Google cofounders Larry Page and Sergey Brin, departed between the proposal's announcement and Dec. 31, 2025—the day before the liability would take effect.

These departures alone reduce the measure's supposed tax revenue by nearly 40 percent before a single dollar is collected. Once migration patterns uncovered in the academic literature are applied to quieter departures, expected revenue falls to only $40 billion.

Now, factor in the normal state taxes that will no longer be collected from departing billionaires. Rauh's team calculates that by shrinking the existing tax base, the measure's "net present value" is at least a $25 billion loss for California.

Then there is the retroactivity problem. The proposal aims to tax billionaires based on residency and conduct that reaches back to January 1, long before any vote was cast. Individuals who believe they lawfully established residency elsewhere might have to fight California in court for years (at the expense of the remaining taxpayers), based on details as arbitrary as where these billionaires kept their pets or held club memberships.

The "onetime" framing of the tax deserves equal skepticism. As Rauh points out, the measure includes a constitutional authorization to lift California's cap on taxation of intangible personal property. Once that legal infrastructure exists, future wealth taxes can be imposed at any rate, at any threshold, at any time. It is, in other words, a permanent new power for the state.

The Billionaire Tax Act is so erratic and its precedent so problematic that it practically begs Californians to pay attention to the second ballot measure. All Americans' savings should be safe from such confiscation based on three clear principles.

First, fairness: When a worker sets aside after-tax income to invest for retirement, the resulting balance is not untapped revenue. To treat this savings as a fresh tax base is to tax the same dollar twice.

Second, stability: A tax system that reaches into asset values rather than income flows is inherently volatile. A founder whose stock drops 40 percent in a downturn still owes wealth tax on last year's greater valuation. An ordinary saver whose 401(k) is taxed would face the same absurdity.

Third, and most urgent, is California's own track record. According to the state's nonpartisan Legislative Analyst's Office, state spending is poised to grow by nearly 70 percent between 2019 and the coming fiscal year, drastically outpacing a significant revenue hike over the period. The result is a cumulative deficit exceeding $50 billion over the next two years, a hole entirely of Sacramento's own making, unrelated to Washington. Trusting politicians with that spending record to stop at taxing billionaires is reckless and naive.

When the wealth tax inevitably fails to deliver, the state will look for the next available pool of assets. Nonbillionaires who remain after California's billionaires depart will be the likely targets, and their retirement savings could be the new tax base. As Rauh wrote earlier this month in his ongoing exploration of the proposals, "While approximately 0.001% of California households are billionaires, approximately 62% have retirement accounts."

If this prediction sounds far-fetched due to federal protections—or if you think billionaires will always be treated differently than normal savers who fill retirement accounts over a lifetime—consider what California already does to health savings accounts (HSAs).

Federal law treats HSA contributions and earnings as tax-exempt. But under California's tax engineering, the interest, dividends, and capital gains are treated as ordinary income, affecting roughly 4.5 million residents. These people are not billionaires or millionaires. Politicians simply decided this was revenue the state was entitled to tax. Doing the same with 401(k)s and IRAs would not require new principles, just the same willingness.

A wealth tax on billionaires is the first step, and it puts the retirement savings of ordinary Californians at risk. The HSA precedent suggests that the threat is real. The Retirement and Personal Savings Protection Act would erect constitutional barriers against exactly that kind of expansion.

COPYRIGHT 2026 CREATORS.COM

The post How the Billionaire Tax Could Make California Poorer appeared first on Reason.com.

articles.title: A Prescient Poem About AI
articles.content:
一篇文章戏谑地将人工智能比作我们的孩子，这让我想起了菲利普·拉金的这节诗：

他们把你搞糟了，你的爹和娘。
他们可能并非有意，但他们确实这样。
他们把自身的毛病都塞进你身上，
还另外添了些，就为了你。

这篇文章《一首关于人工智能的有先见之明的诗》首次发表于Reason.com。
articles.summary:
A post jocularly characterizing AIs as our children reminded me of this stanza from Philip Larkin:

They fuck you up, your mum and dad.
They may not mean to, but they do.
They fill you with the faults they had
And add some extra, just for you.

The post A Prescient Poem About AI appeared first on Reason.com.
